items.q: What ending does an adjective take in 'der gute Mann' (the good man)?
items.why: Students apply noun gender to the adjective directly. Adjective endings depend on whether the article already shows case/gender. After der (which marks case), the adjective takes a weak -e.

items.q: Where does the main verb go in 'Ich kann gut Deutsch sprechen'?
items.correct: At the end of the clause (sprechen).
items.why: Students place the infinitive next to the modal. German modals push the main verb's INFINITIVE to the END: 'Ich kann gut Deutsch SPRECHEN' - the modal (kann) is in V2 position; the infinitive goes last.

items.q: When addressing a stranger or someone older formally, which 'you' do you use?
items.correct: Sie (always capitalised, formal singular and plural).
items.why: Students default to du. German has THREE 'you' forms: du (informal singular), ihr (informal plural), Sie (formal singular OR plural - capitalised). Using du with a stranger or older person is rude. Sie takes third-person plural verb forms.
The answer examiners want is: 1 Sept 1939. “3 Sept 1939” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's war gegangen. “ist gegangen” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: On what day in May 1945 did Germany surrender?
items.why: Exact answer: 8 May. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
